---
title: "Practice-based Education Data Science: A Proposal for Our Emerging Field"
subtitle: "A keynote presentation at the Stanford Education Data Science conference"
author: "Joshua M. Rosenberg, Ph.D."
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [simple, custom.scss]
    slide-number: c/t
    incremental: false
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    transition: none
    auto-stretch: false
    code-block-bg: false
---

# What is Education Data Science, Really?

## What do you _mean_ by ...

<div style="text-align:center">
<p class="fragment"><em><strong>My goal is to improve educational <u>outcomes</u>.</strong></em></p>
<p class="fragment"><em><strong>My goal is to <u>improve</u> educational outcomes.</strong></em></p>
<p class="fragment"><em><strong>My <u>goal</u> is to improve educational outcomes.</strong></em></p>
</div>

::: {.notes}
I have a friend, and maybe you have a friend like this (or this person is you!) who is always interrogating and critiquing the subtle framing of research. If I say, for example, my goal is to improve educational outcomes, they will ask, of course:

which outcomes? A totally reasonable question. 

But then they will also ask, what do you mean by improve? A harder question.

And, even, what do you mean by "my goal"? At this point, I often found myself provoked and ready to abandon the conversation, but, on reflection, the questions are always thoughtful. 

This talk is meant (or should I say, I mean for this talk!) to get a bit at something related - what are the underlying goals of what we call educational data science?

By practice-based Education Data Science, I mean work where educational practice is not merely the site of application, but a source of problems, standards of usefulness, and accountability.
:::

## The question motivating this talk

<div style="text-align:center; font-size: 1.3em;">

***Is Education Data Science a methodological program that takes education as a use case — or is educational practice a source of problems, standards, and accountability?***

</div>

# My pathway to the question

## My background --- science teaching!

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="60%"}
![](/img/jr-classroom.jpg){width="100%"}
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
![](/img/schoolpicture.bmp){width="80%"}
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
I'll share very briefly where I am coming from. I was a science teacher, and I intended for that to be my career. I am still deeply tied to K-12 education and K-12 science education. I am PI for an NSF CAREER project that - broadly - supports science teachers to take up and to teach about data science ideas. My wife is a librarian working at a public school and so I engage, well, deeply through her and that work.
:::

## Seeing my students use Excel

![](/img/excel.jpg){width="70%"}

## Seeing peers create a simple visualization through code

![](/img/word-cloud-fuglei.png){width="70%"}

[From [the Resilient Educator](https://resilienteducator.com/classroom-resources/word-clouds-writing/)]{.smaller}

::: {.notes}

I came to data science through science education. I think of the spark being seeing students changing the colors of graphs they made in Excel, and seeing them relate to their product --- a small but meaningful relationship to data; claiming a relationship.

Another was seeing a colleague create an oft- (and I think correctly) bemoaned data visualization of text data, a word cloud. But they were so proud they created - coded - it. 

Still another was laying in bed in graduate school looking at R code I wrote, that worked. It was eye opening; I wasn't trained as a programmer, but I became, over time, interested, and then a bit obsessed, for years reading every book I could find (I think literally) on R, statistics using R.
:::

## Teacher education

![](/img/utk.jpeg){width="70%"}

## Software

![](/img/tidylpa-website.png){width="70%"}

::: {.notes}

hard to explain to colleagues!
:::


## DSIEUR

![](/img/dsieur-cover.jpg){width="70%"}

::: {.notes}

easier to explain

not the best programmer, not the best teacher educator
:::

## CREDIBLE

![](/img/credible-group.jpg){width="70%"}

# Turn and talk

<div style="text-align:center; font-size: 1em;">

***What was your path to education data science?***

</div>

# Three pressures make this choice urgent

::: {.notes}

There are a lot! Let's highlight 3

:::

## Funding cuts and the need to show impact

![](/img/nsf%20edu%20funding.png){width="70%"}

[From [Grant Witness](https://grant-witness.us/funding_curves_nsf.html)]{.smaller}

## Educator and student data needs unmet

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="60%"}
> "I really do not understand the statistical stuff. I might as well be reading Russian."

> "It really freaks me out. I am not strong at math or with numbers."

> "Some of the programs, such as R, are a little intimidating."
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
**Technical factors** were the most common source of intimidation:

*37 of 44 participants (84%)*

:::

::::

[Staudt Willet and Rosenberg, 2025](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s41686-023-00083-7){.smaller}

## Educator and student data needs unmet

![](/img/tech%20tools.png){width="70%"}

[Rosenberg et al. (2022)](https://bera-journals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/pdfdirect/10.1111/bjet.13245){.smaller}

## Educator and student data needs unmet

![](/img/barriers.png){width="70%"}

[Rosenberg et al. (2022)](https://bera-journals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/pdfdirect/10.1111/bjet.13245){.smaller}

## Allied fields are growing

![](/img/eds-growth-abstract.png){width="70%"}

[Drawing on McFarland et al. (2021)](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/pdf/10.1177/23328584211052055){.smaller}

[Through 2025]{.smaller}

# Cleveland's Challenge to Statisticians (and Us)

## What impacts the work of the data analyst?

<p class="fragment"><em><strong>"An action plan to enlarge the technical areas of statistics focuses on the data analyst."</strong></em></p>

<p class="fragment"><em><strong>"The basic premise is that technical areas of data science should be judged by the extent to which they enable the analyst to learn from data."</strong></em></p>

<p class="fragment"><em><strong>"The benefit of an area can be direct or indirect."</strong></em></p>

<p class="fragment"><em><strong>"Tools that are used by the data analyst are of direct benefit. Theories that serve as a basis for developing tools are of indirect benefit."</strong></em></p>

<p class="fragment"><a href="https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/j.1751-5823.2001.tb00477.x" style="font-size: 0.5em;">Cleveland (2001)</a></p>

## How much time and how many resources should be dedicated to different areas of data science?

::: {style="font-size: 1.4em; text-align: center;"}
| Area | Share |
|---|---|
| Multidisciplinary Investigations | 25% |
| Models and Methods for Data | 20% |
| Theory | 20% |
| Computing with Data | 15% |
| Pedagogy | 15% |
| Tool Evaluation | 5% |
:::

[Cleveland (2001)](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/j.1751-5823.2001.tb00477.x){.smaller}

## If education is the domain

What if we judged our field by **how much teachers, learners, and leaders in educational systems can learn from data**?

I argue that this is practice-based Education Data Science

What would the areas for an Education Data Science discipline (or program or department) be?

# Five kinds of work this orientation makes visible

:::{.notes}
**These can bring us closer to educational practice, be distinct from "general" data science, and be an area of comparative advantage.**
:::

## These are just examples --- it's up to us to flesh out

1. Right-sized data problems
1. Data science education infrastructure
1. Research-practice partnerships around data education
1. Thinking about impact beyond journals articles
1. Teaching practitioners and researchers

## Right-sized data problems

Blair (Higher Education; Administrator): *A lot of my job is taking data that the school generates and helping the students to better understand their progress and also helping the faculty to better understand how to assess students’ progress and better support them... ***It’s not even a very sophisticated analysis, but it's aggregating it together and presenting it to them in a way that they can take action on it.**

[Staudt Willet and Rosenberg, in preparation]{.smaller}

:::{.notes}

Jamie (Industry, Learning Analytics): "And it's so so simple, **almost boringly simple.**"

:::

## Right-sized data problems

The work of an educational data scientist is **“extremely delicate because it simultaneously necessitates the technical capacity for sophisticated data analysis and the sensibility for the specificities of the educational field” (p. 198).**

[Agasisti and Bowers (2017)](https://www.elgaronline.com/edcollchap/edcoll/9781785369063/9781785369063.00014.xml){.smaller}

## Data science education infrastructure

<p class="fragment">A data science department in a university must, of course, concern itself with teaching in its own setting. <strong>But it is vital that resources be spent to study pedagogy and to teach pedagogy.</strong></p>

<p class="fragment">It makes sense that such study encompass more than the university setting; <strong>curricula in elementary and secondary schools, company training programs, and continuing education programs are important as well.</strong></p>

<p class="fragment">Education in data science does many things. <strong>It trains statisticians. But just as important it trains non-statisticians, conveying how valuable data science is for learning about the world.</strong></p>

<p class="fragment"><a href="https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/j.1751-5823.2001.tb00477.x" style="font-size: 0.5em;">Cleveland (2001)</a></p>

## Data science education infrastructure

![](/img/state%20tiers.png){width="70%"}

## Data science education infrastructure

![](/img/program%20adoption.png){width="70%"}

<!-- NOTE TO SELF:
This can be part of “data science education infrastructure.”
Claim: educational data scientists are especially well positioned here because data education is our purview in the way laboratory investigations are partly biologists’ purview.
Could include tools, curricula, assessments, PD, and learning environments for data science education.

This may be the explicit Cleveland bridge.
Not: “Cleveland mentioned pedagogy, so we should mention teacher education.”
Rather: Cleveland shows how a field can ask what kinds of work its domain requires. Then ask that question for education.
-->

## Research-practice partnerships

![](/img/leads%20ds.png){width="70%"}

## Thinking about impact beyond journal articles

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](/img/cepa.png){width="100%"}
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](/img/ETB.png){width="100%"}
:::

::::

## Teaching practitioners and researchers

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](/img/buffalo.png){width="100%"}
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](/img/cade.png){width="100%"}
:::

::::

## A few words on AI (that haven't been said already)

AI _can_ bring us **closer to practice** —

or it can make it easier to **never engage with it at all**.

# Back to the core question

<div style="text-align:center; font-size: 1.3em;">

***Is Education Data Science a methodological program that takes education as a use case — or is educational practice a source of problems, standards, and accountability?***

</div>

# Turn and talk 

<div style="text-align:center; font-size: 1em;">

***If we are to judge our field by how much teachers, learners, and leaders in educational systems can learn from data, what are the areas of work that we should be doing? 
***

</div>

# One worked example

## Starting with practitioner needs for water quality data

![](/img/Image-1.jpg){width="70%"}

## Shiny + CODAP

![](/img/cld-full.png){width="70%"}

## It failed

![](/img/cld-class-bug.jpg){width="70%"}

## Then we fixed it 

![](/img/science-classroom.jpg){width="70%"}

## Students figured something out that was not known by others

![](/img/e coli.jpg){width="70%"}

# Conclusion

## Summarizing the argument 

One option is that Education Data Science is simply a methodological program 

Another is that Education Data Science becomes something other than data science applied in education when educational practice is the focus of the work

My argument is that the latter _does work that a general data scientist would struggle to do alone_ and that that the latter has the potential to impact education in a more sustained way

## What is part of practice-based Education Data Science?

How seriously is education practice taken? **This can take many different forms --- direct and indirect**

*A practical test*: what would this look like in a school or in a district office?

## A call (to myself and others)

Choose problems close enough to educational practice that its answers matter.

Value work that emphasizes impact, partnership, and the data-related problems teachers, students, and others in education face.

For more junior scholars, do work that shows us what this looks like!

## Acknowledgements 

Project CREDIBLE teachers around Knox County Schools, especially teacher leaders and Jen Sauer

Zhen Xu, Cody Pritchard, Leah Rosenbaum, and Bradley Holtz at the University of Tennessee Knoxville

This material is based upon work supported by the National Science Foundation under Grant No. 2239152.
